<!-- Converted from PDF to Markdown. Tables are preserved primarily as extracted text blocks; embedded figures are included as base64 data URIs for portability. -->

# A hybrid framework for improving students’ performance prediction based on CNN with Bi-LSTM

**Abstract:** Accurately predicting how well students will perform is critical, as it hinges on numerous
factors. Machine learning and data mining tools offer powerful solutions for this task. This paper delves
into the use of XGBoost, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machines, Support Vector Machines, and
Convolutional Neural Networks for predicting student success. Three datasets from various levels (school,
college, and online) with diverse input parameters are used to compare the effectiveness of these five
techniques. The paper not only presents comparative results but also explores how their performance
changes with different tuning parameters. By evaluating their capabilities across three distinct datasets,
the study reveals that Decision tree and CNN with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory outperform
the other three methods.
**Keywords:** XGBoost, Data preprocessing, Machine Learning, Deep learning, Convolution Neural Network,
Predict student performance.

## 1 Introduction
In the context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, the burgeoning era of Industry 4.0, the realms of machine
learning and deep learning have emerged as focal points capturing the attention of researchers. This technological
wave has permeated diverse fields, such as smart energy management [21],[21, 22, 32], computer vision [35, 37, 30],
software management [11, 20], social network analysis [36, 38, 15], business intelligence [26, 31], medical informatics
[5, 3] , and structural engineering [28, 29]. Notably, the field of education has witnessed a significant expansion of
research endeavors aimed at enhancing various facets of teaching methodologies. Within the educational domain,
the application of machine learning, particularly the utilization of Large Language Models (LLMs), has gained
considerable popularity. These applications extend to areas such as augmenting students’ knowledge and predicting
student dropouts. This article delves into the exploration of predicting student achievement, aiming to facilitate the
development of tailored teaching methods for individual students. Through our research, we endeavor to contribute
valuable insights that will enrich the landscape of educational practices.
In recent years, we have witnessed an unprecedented surge in research focused on the dynamic realms of machine
learning and deep learning. Notably, within the domain of deep learning, the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
algorithm has gained remarkable prominence across various research endeavors. Illustratively, K M Sajjadul Islam
et al [18] harnessed LSTM’s capabilities for natural language processing, specifically addressing autocorrection tasks
related to Chief Complaints within Electronic Health Records. Furthermore, Huafeng Qin et al [24] explored the
extensive utility of LSTM in their innovative work on Mix Transformer, particularly in the context of eye movement
recognition. In tandem with LSTM, the Bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) has emerged as a versatile tool, finding
applications in diverse fields. For instance, Dhrubajyoti Pathak et al [23] leveraged Bi-LSTM for speech processing
within the realm of natural language processing. Yuanzhe Jia et al [19] extended the application of Bi-LSTM to
the domain of stock price prediction, showcasing the algorithm’s adaptability across distinct domains.
Beyond the confines of deep learning, traditional machine learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machines
(SVM), XGBoost [4], and Decision Trees [34] have been instrumental in processing tabular data. The literature
abounds with research articles presenting comparative analyses between deep learning architectures and traditional
algorithms. For instance, Juan Esteban Berger’s 2023 study delved into a nuanced comparison between Neural
Networks and XGBoost, specifically in predicting Pricing European Options. In a parallel vein, Abu Bakar Siddiqur
Rahman et al [25] contributed a benchmark study in the arena of text classification, pitting machine learning

stalwarts like SVM, XGBoost, and Light GBM against deep learning counterparts such as BERT, GAN-BERT, and
BART.
In panel data analysis, tackling data imbalance is vital, and oversampling methods like SMOTE and ADASYN
play key roles in equalizing class distributions and averting training biases. SMOTE generates synthetic instances
for the minority class, while ADASYN adapts the process to focus on challenging instances. These methods not
only balance datasets but also enhance model discernment within minority classes, boosting predictive accuracy.
Our paper delves into the application of SMOTE, ADASYN, and similar techniques in panel data analysis, offering
insights into addressing data imbalance challenges effectively.
The remainder of this article is structured as follows. An overview of related works is given in Section 2, which
focuses on studies of the data imbalance problem, solutions, and applications. The experiment for the classification
of student achievement is presented in Section 3. Analizing the result are described in Section 4. Finally, Section 5
summarizes the results of this study and offers several directions for future work.

## 2 Related work
### 2.1    Feature selection
Feature selection is the process of identifying the most pertinent characteristics in the dataset and then using
machine learning methods to improve the model’s performance. An excessive amount of unnecessary characteristics
makes training take considerably longer and raises the possibility of overfitting.
The Pearson correlation coefficient, denoted by the symbol r, is a widely used statistical measure that quantifies
the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables. It ranges from -1 to 1, with
a value of -1 indicating a perfect negative correlation (as one variable increases, the other decreases), a value of 0
indicating no correlation, and a value of +1 indicating a perfect positive correlation (as one variable increases, the
other also increases). The Pearson correlation coefficient is calculated using the following formula:
n
P
(Xi − X̄)(Yi − Ȳ )
i=1
r= s
n
P                  n
P
(Xi − X̄)2       (Yi − Ȳ )2
i=1                i=1

In which, Xi and Yi are the individual values of the two variables, X̄ and Ȳ are the mean values of the two variables.

### 2.2    Oversampling Technique
When faced with imbalanced data, where one class far outnumbers the rest, traditional machine learning models
can bias. Oversampling techniques take a bold stance, directly addressing the imbalance by boosting the number
of data points in the underrepresented minority class. This can be achieved through various methods, from copying
existing minority samples to generating entirely new synthetic ones. By increasing the minority’s presence, we
tilt the scales, forcing the model to pay attention to its often-ignored patterns and relationships. This balanced
training data yields improved model accuracy and fairness, allowing us to gain meaningful insights even from skewed
datasets.
The Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) is a preprocessing strategy used to address class
imbalance in datasets [6] . It tackles this issue by artificially generating new data points for the minority class,
balancing the representation of both classes and improving the performance of machine learning models trained on
imbalanced data. SMOTE achieves this by interpolating between existing minority class data points, effectively
creating new synthetic data points that are similar to the existing ones. This process helps to mitigate the bias
towards the majority class and allows models to learn more effectively from the minority class, leading to improved
overall performance and more reliable predictions.
Predicting outcomes with imbalanced datasets, where one class significantly outnumbers others, poses a challenge
for traditional machine learning algorithms. They tend to favor the majority class, neglecting and misinterpreting

the crucial signals hidden within the underrepresented minority. To address this bias, a powerful technique called
Adaptive Synthetic Sampling (ADASYN) [16] emerges.
ADASYN tackles the imbalance by intelligently generating synthetic data points for the minority class. It
focuses on hard-to-learn examples within the minority, effectively amplifying their presence in the training data.
This targeted approach enhances the model’s ability to recognize patterns and relationships specific to the minority
class, ultimately leading to more accurate and robust predictions despite the imbalance. By boosting the voice of
the underrepresented.
Imbalanced datasets, where rare but critical minority classes are overshadowed by dominant majorities, pose a
unique challenge for machine learning. One powerful approach to tackle this is the marriage of Synthetic Minority
Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) with Support Vector Machines (SVM) [14]. SMOTE addresses the imbalance by
strategically generating synthetic data points for the minority class, augmenting the training data without biasing
towards existing examples. This enriched data then fuels the powerful SVM algorithm, which excels at finding
clear decision boundaries even in complex, high-dimensional spaces. The synergy between SMOTE and SVM
unlocks enhanced class separability, particularly for the minority class, yielding improved classification accuracy
and robustness, even in the face of significant imbalance. This combined approach paves the way for reliable and
insightful analysis in diverse fields where imbalanced data is the norm.

### 2.3    Model structure
We build experiments based on 8 different models.
The Support Vector Machine (SVM) was developed by Vapnik for binary classification [8]. The simplest
way to extend SVMs for multiclass prob-lems is using the so-called one-vs-rest approach (Vapnik [33], 1995). It
is used to find hyperplanes in an N-dimensional space that divides data into two parts corresponding to their
class. The SVM algorithm helps to find the best line or decision boundary, this best boundary or region is called
a hyperplane. The SVM algorithm finds the closest point of the lines from both classes. These points are called
support vectors. The distance between the vectors and the hyperplane is called a margin. The goal of this algorithm
is to find the most beautiful hyperplane which has the maximum margin to divide the data layer.
In SVM, a kernel is a function that implicitly maps the input data into a high-dimensional feature space. This
allows SVM to handle non-linear relationships between features and the target variable, which would be impossible
to directly model in the original low-dimensional space. In this paper, we test on three models: linear, radial basis
of polynomial.
Random Forest is a Supervised Machine Learning Algorithm used in classification and regression problems
[12]. It can be understood that a Random Forest is a type of model consisting of a set of decision trees, each decision
tree is generated based on random rules and data sets from the sample data set. Predictive results are based on a
majority vote for classification and average in the case of regression.
XGBoost is a scalable and improved version of gradient boosting [7]. XGBoost works by using an ensemble of
decision trees, which are models that construct a graph of ”if” statements based on the input features. XGBoost
builds the decision trees sequentially, adding one tree at a time that tries to correct the errors of the previous
trees. XGBoost uses the gradient of a loss function that measures how well the model fits the data to guide the
construction of new trees.
Gradient boosting machines (GBM) are powerful ensemble models combining multiple weak learners,
typically decision trees, to achieve strong predictive performance [13]. Each tree in the ensemble focuses on correcting
the errors made by its predecessors, iteratively building a more accurate model. This sequential learning process,
guided by the gradient of the loss function, allows GBM to capture complex relationships and non-linear interactions
within data, making them versatile tools for diverse tasks like regression, classification, and ranking. Their inherent
interpretability through individual tree analysis also provides valuable insights into the model’s decision-making
process. By fine-tuning hyperparameters like tree depth and learning rate, GBM can be optimized for specific
datasets and tasks, further solidifying their position as a robust and adaptable solution for modern machine learning
applications.
A type of deep feed-forward artificial neural network called a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is

frequently employed to solve computer vision issues like picture categorization. CNN may be distinguished from a
”plain” multilayer perceptron (MLP) network by using non-linearities like tanh, sigmoid, and ReLU in addition to
pooling and convolutional layers.
The convolutional layer (abbreviated CONV) is made up of a filter, for example, 5x5x1 (5 pixels for width and
height, and 1 since the pictures are grayscale). By ”sliding” over an input image’s width and height, the CONV
layer, intuitively, is used to calculate the dot product of the input’s region and the weight learning parameters. As a
result, a 2-dimensional activation map with the filter’s reactions at certain places will be created. As a consequence,
the pooling layer, represented by POOL, shrinks the input picture sizes in accordance with the CONV filter’s
output. This reduces the number of parameters in the model as well; this process is known as down-sampling.

### 2.4    Evaluation metrics
Performance evaluation metrics are the quantifiable measures used to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of a
model. They provide a standardized way to compare and analyze different models or evaluate the performance of
a single model under different conditions.
Confusion Matrix is an N × N matrix used for evaluating performance of a classification model, where N is
the number of target classes. The matrix compares the actual target values with those predicted by the machine
learning model. Assuming confusion matrix for binary class which is either ‘Single’ or ‘Married’ representation will
be as follows:
Predicted value
Yes      No
Yes      TP       FN
Actual value
No       FP       TN

Table 1: Confusion matrix

In Table 4, True Positive (TP) - When both predicted class and actual class are Yes, False Negative (FN) -
When class is predicted to be No but actual class is Yes, False Positive (FP) - When class is predicted to be Yes
but actual class is No, True Negative (TN) - When both predicted class and actual class are No.
Accuracy is the ratio between the number of correct predictions and the total number of predictions.
(T P + T N )
Accuracy =
(T P + F P + T N + F N )
Precision is the ratio of the total number of correctly classified positive classes divided by the total number of
predicted positive classes.
(T P )
P recision =
(T P + F P )
Recall is a measure of actual observations which are predicted correctly. It is also known as Sensitivity.
(T P
Recall =
(T P + F N s)
F1 Score is a number between 0 and 1 and is the harmonic mean of precision and recall.
2 ∗ (P recision ∗ Recall)
Accuracy =
(T P + F P + T N + F N )

## 3 Experiment
### 3.1    Dataset construction
Gathering from the UCI ML Repository, Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset[9] has 395 instances with
33 characteristics, of which the attribute ’G3’ is selected as the prediction object. Visualize with a column chart
the specific data points divided into 5 classes of the ’G3’ attribute presented in Figure 1

Figure 1: Visualization of Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset

![Figure 1](data:image/png;base64,)

With the Student Performance in Portuguese language Dataset[9], there is a total of 649 instances, including
33 attributes, of which the attribute ’G3’ is selected as the prediction object. Visualize with a column chart the
specific data points divided into 5 classes of the ’G3’ attribute presented in Figure 2

Figure 2: Visualization of Student Performance in Portuguese language Dataset

![Figure 2](data:image/png;base64,)

Finally, we experiment on Students’ Academic Performance Dataset[2]. This dataset includes 480 instances and
16 attributes, in which the predicted labels are divided into 3 classes shown in the Figure 3.

### 3.2    Experimental setup
All of the experiments in this study were implemented on Google Colaboratory with version 3.10.12 of the
Python programming language and GPU T4.
In this section, we introduce the overall architecture, consisting preprocessing, splitting data, oversampling,
classification and evaluation. The general architecture is presented in Figure 4.
Prior to model training, to ensure our model effectively handled categorical features, we adopted one-hot en-
coding during data preprocessing. This transformed each unique category into a separate numberic data, allowing
the model to learn distinct relationships with each category and capture complex interactions.
We set up feature selection with Pearson Correlation. First we will calculate the Pearson Correlation. Which
attributes (columns) have sigps >= 0.6 or sigps <= 0.6 in Pearson Correlation, we will keep this attribute, if
abs(sigps ) <= 0.6 we will remove this attribute.
After removing attributes identified as uncorrelated, we normalize the data using the Min-max scaling method

Figure 3: Visualization of Students’ Academic Performance Dataset

![Figure 3](data:image/png;base64,)

for the attributes (except predicted features). Next, we divide these datasets into training sets and testing sets
using Random Sampling and Fractional Sampling floor. Before training, we will set up three cases: Repeated Cross-
validation, oversampling with SMOTE, oversampling with ADASYN, and oversampling with Borderline SMOTE
SVM for machine learning algorithms (Support vector machine, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting Machine, and Random
forest), for convolution neural networks we only experiment on the case of Repeated Cross-validation.

Figure 4: General structure

![Figure 4](data:image/png;base64,)

In the quest for optimal performance, hyperparameter tuning plays a crucial role. Grid search, a foundational
approach, systematically explores a predefined grid of parameter combinations, training and evaluating a model with
each. This exhaustive search, akin to traversing a map, identifies the combination that yields the best results, guiding
us to the pinnacle of model performance within the explored landscape. Though computationally demanding, grid
search offers a reliable roadmap for navigating the hyperparameter wilderness, ensuring we reach the peak of model
effectiveness. To facilitate parameter tuning, we use grid search for Machine learning algorithms.
With support vector machines, the table presents the grid search parameters used with this algorithm between
three different kernels: linear, polynomial, and radial basis function. Table 2 shows the parameters for the linear
kernel.
With the radial basis function kernel, the parameter gamma is an important hyperparameter that controls the
model’s ability to capture nonlinear relationships in the data. The search grid table for this algorithm is presented
in Table 3.
Finally, we test the SVM algorithm with poly kernel. In addition to the 3 indexes mentioned above, degree
determines the degree of the polynomial kernel used by the SVM. All parameters are presented in Table 3.

Hyperparameter                             Value
C                       0.01       0.1    1     10
decision function shape                   ovo              ovr

Table 2: Parametric grid search table for the support vector machine algorithm with linear kernel

Hyperparameter                             Value
C                       0.01       0.1    1     10
gramma                      scale            auto
decision function shape                   ovo              ovr

Table 3: Parametric grid search table for the support vector machine algorithm with radial basis function kernel

Hyperparameter                             Value
C                       0.01       0.1    1     10
gramma                      scale            auto
degree                     1         2      3
decision function shape                   ovo              ovr

Table 4: parametric grid search table for the support vector machine algorithm with poly kernel

Table 5 presents the grid search parameters used with the Random forest algorithm with criterion determines
the function used to measure the quality of a split in a decision tree. Common options like Gini impurity and
entropy assess how effectively a split reduces uncertainty or improves class separation. The chosen criterion can
influence the tree’s structure and ultimately the model’s performance. min samples split defines the minimum
number of samples required to split an internal node. min samples leaf specifies the minimum number of samples
required to be at a leaf node. Finally, max f eatures controls the buffet, limiting the number of features considered
at each split.

Hyperparameter                                     Value
criterion            ’gini’,       ’entropy’         ’log loss’
min samples split           2               3                 4
min samples leaf            1               2                 3         4   5
max features                      sqrt                      log2

Table 5: Parametric grid search table for the Random forest machines algorithm

With XGBoost, table 6 presents the grid search parameters used with the this algorithm with gamma specifies
minimum loss reduction required to create a new split in a tree, eta defines the learning rate used to update
the weights of the trees in each boosting iteration, max depth controls the maximum depth of the trees in the
XGBoost model, colsample bytree specifies the fraction of features randomly sampled for splitting at each tree
node, min child weight defines the minimum sum of weights (hessian) in a child node and subsample specifies the
fraction of training instances randomly sampled for building each tree.
Table 7 presents the grid search parameters used with the GBM algorithm with learning rate controls how
quickly the model adapts its weights during training, criterion determines the function used to measure model
performance during training, maxdepth defines the maximum depth allowed for decision trees or tree-based ensemble
models.
In this article, we experiment the model according to 2 Convolution Neural network (CNN) architectures (Tabel

Hyperparamer                               Value
max depth            2            3        4          5   6
eta              0.001        0.01     0.1
gamma               0.5          1       1.5         2   5
colsample bytree          0.3         0.6      0.8     1.0
min child weight          1            5       10
subsample                  0.5                 0.75

Table 6: Parametric grid search table for the XGBoost algorithm

Hyperparamer                                 Value
learning rate       0.001          0.01         0.1
criterion         ’friedman mse’              ’squared error’
max depth            1                2         3      4       5

Table 7: Parametric grid search table for the Gradient boosting machines algorithm

18) including CNN combined with LSTM layer[17] and CNN combined with Bi-LSTM. Details of the structure are
presented in the table 8

Dataset student Math
Dataset xAPI
and student Portuguese
CNN with             CNN with                    CNN with               CNN with
LSTM               BiLSTM                       LSTM                   BiLSTM
4 x Conv1D          4 x Conv1D                  4 x Conv1D               4 x Conv1D

Layer
LSTM               Bi-LSTM                   4 x Conv1D               4 x Conv1D

Finally, we analyze and evaluate the outputs with several common metrics such as Accuracy, Recall, Precision
and F1-Score. These metrics are computed as follows in section 2.4

### 3.3    Experimental results
In this section, we present the results of feature selection of the 3 datasets presented above.
First, we experimented with selecting attributes with Pearson correlation. Table 9 presents the top 5 attributes
with the highest scores. Parameters of attributes that satisfy the conditions presented in part 3 are printed in bold
in table 9.
After observation, attributes G1 and G2 had satisfied the conditions mentioned above, so we decided to choose
those two indicators for experimentation.
With a larger number of attributes than Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset, table 10 shows the
top 5 attributes with the highest index with the Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset data set. In which,
the attributes shown in bold are the attributes that meet the requirements.

Top     Feature     Pearson correlation
1        G2                0.941
2        G1                0.865
3     failures            -0.317
4      Medu               0.225
5    schoolsup            -0.180

Table 9: Top 5 feature have highest Pearson correlation of Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset

Top     Feature     Pearson correlation
1       G2               0.918
2       G1               0.853
3     failures           -0.370
4      higher             0.330
5      Medu               0.282

Table 10: Top 5 feature have highest Pearson correlation of Student Performance in Portuguese language Dataset

Similarly, we can see that the attributes G1 and G2 satisfy the description in section 3. Therefore, we decided
to choose those two indicators for experimentation.
Finally with the Students’ Academic Performance Dataset dataset, we illustrate the top 5 attributes in table 11
in which the attributes raisedhands, V isitedResources, StudentAbsenceDays have outstanding pearson correlation
index and satisfy the condition presented (Section III).

Top            Feature            Pearson correlation
1       VisitedResources                   0.677
2     StudentAbsenceDays                   -0.671
3           raisedhands                    0.646
4      AnnouncementsView                   0.527
5     ParentAnsweringSurvey                0.435

Table 11: Top 5 feature have highest Pearson correlation of Students’ Academic Performance Dataset

In this section we will compare and analyze the results achieved. The outstanding results are retrieved and
analyzed, all remaining results are left in appendix.
As we have seen, our data has differences between different attributes. With experiments without using
oversampling with Decision Tree, the accuracy achieved 93.67%. Figure 5 below shows an image of the decision
tree’s confusion matrix without using the oversampling method. Through the confusion matrix, we can see the
error especially in classes with fewer samples than the remaining samples, that’s why we apply 3 types of oversam-
pling techniques including SMOTE, ADASYN, Borderline-SMOTE SVM to improve the above situation, in which
SMOTE gives the best results.
The experimental results after using SMOTE with the first 4 algorithms including Decision Tree, Random
forest, Gradient Boosting Machine and Xgboost with the Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset are presented
in the table 12. With the results of the SVM algorithm, we describe in table 13.
With the above results, among all types of machine learning algorithms, Decision tree gives the best results
with an accuracy of 96.2%.

Figure 5: Visualization of the confusion matrix of Decision tree in Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset

![Figure 5](data:image/png;base64,)

Decision       Random
GBM      XGBoost
Tree         forest
Training set   Accuracy      75.63%        75.63%      79.25%     77.14%
Accuracy       96.2%        94.94%      83.54%     81.01%
Precision      94%         92.82%      84.21%     77.42%
Testing set
Recall      96.24%        95.65%      83.81%     76.62%
F1-Score      94.5%        93.66%      82.51%     76.49%

Table 12: Results table of first 4 algorithms of Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset.

SVM - linear      SVM - poly        SVM - rbf
Training set   Accuracy         78.16%              76.44%        77.71%
Accuracy         84.81%              81.01%        81.01%
Precision        82.80%              81.93%        77.37%
Testing set
Recall         82.61%              81.10%        76.75%
F1-Score         82.37%              78.16%        75.75%

Table 13: Results table of Support Vector Machine algorithms of Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset.

In term of the results of combining CNN with LSTM and Bi-LSTM, the results of CNN with Bi-LSTM have
a impressive improvement, the training process is performed on the Tensorflow show in figures 6 and showing the
acuuracy and loss parameters respectively.
As we can see, the accuracy of both the train and test sets increased rapidly within the first 20 epochs and
reached the highest accuracy index of 100% for the test set. Besides, the Dropout layer is added to reduce overfitting,
so we can see that the accuracy index of the training set is smaller than the testing set. In opposite, the loss index
(figure 7) witnessed a significant downward trend in the first 20 epochs.
With previous results, our method achieves better results than C5.0 (Madhuri T. Sathe et al 2021) [27] and
naive predictor (Paulo Cortez et al 2008) [10]. The accuracy comparison results are presented in the table below.
Next, we will present the results using the student poor dataset. Notably, in this episode, we conducted
experiments and achieved optimal outcomes with a dataset that did not involve oversampling. Given the relatively
sufficient number of datasets, the class imbalance is negligible, and the introduction of oversampling algorithms

Accuracy             100%                     98.6%.                78.5%

introduces errors into the data. Table 15 and 16 showcases the superior results obtained by implementing the model
without oversampling. In conclusion, our findings underscore the importance of careful consideration of dataset
characteristics, and in instances of ample data, avoiding oversampling can lead to improved model performance.
With machine learning algorithms, Decision Tree and Random forest give the best results with approximately
89% accuracy. The remaining algorithms are not really impressive with quite low results and overfitting.
With deep learning algorithms, the results are truly impressive. The results presented and compared with the
results of Paulo Cortez at el in table 17 show the overwhelming effectiveness between our results and the previous
paper.
Regrading the xAPI dataset, oversampling with the ADASYN method is quite effective when combined with
machine learning algorithms, producing quite positive results when the accuracy index is superior to other over-

sampling methods. The tables below present the evaluation parameters respectively, with table 18 presenting the

results of SVM.

Through the results, we see that the decision tree gives the best results with an accuracy index of 82.29%.
Next, SVM-linear and XGBoost also produced quite good results with 80.21% and the indices of the remaining
algorithms were in the range of 60% to 70%.
With CNN combined with LSTM and LSTM, we changed it a bit by increasing the training time to 100 epochs
and the results were really impressive when the accuracy index reached up to 84.37%. Moreover, during training,
the accuracy of the oscilloscope test set is shown in figure 8.
Besides, when considering the confusion matrix, we realized that there was a majority of errors in class 2 and
class 3 as shown in figure 9.

SVM-linear    SVM-poly      SVM-rbf
Training set   Accuracy      71.59%         61.89%       92.07%
Accuracy      80.21%         69.79%       67.71%
Precision     80.10%         70.93%       71.28%
Testing set
Recall       81.35%         71.15%       65.47%
F1-Score      80.26%         69.51%       66.37%

Figure 8: Visualization of the accuracy of CNN combine with Bi-LSTM in xAPI Dataset

![Figure 8](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure 9: Visualization of the confusion matrix of CNN combine with Bi-LSTM in xAPI Dataset

![Figure 9](data:image/png;base64,)

Finally, the table 20 compares the results between CNN with LSTM, CNN with BiLSTM and the previously
experimental results of Elaf Abu Amrieh et al [1].
As can be seen, our results are 10% higher than those of Elaf Abu Amrieh et al and show that xAPI combined
with BiLSTM produces truly effective results.

CNN with Bi-LSTM           CNN with LSTM                     ANN
(Our)                    (Our)           (Elaf Abu Amrieh et al)
Accuracy            84.38%                   80.21%                      73.8%
Precision           84.26%                   80.17%                      73.8%
Recall             85.21%                   80.59%                      73.9%
F1-Score            84.47%                   80.33%                      73.2%

Table 20: Evaluation of xAPI dataset

## 4 Conclusion
Building and solving one of the problems of education, predicting the achievement of students, is an issue
that has attracted the attention of many people inside and outside the industry. The achieved result and also
the goal of this project is to find features that have little impact on the predicted object, which is the G3 score
with the Student Performance in Portuguese language Dataset, Student Performance in Mathematics Dataset and
outputClass data sets with xAPI. dataset. The results we achieved are quite impressive with the CNN model
combined with BiLSTM. In particular, not only did we successfully refine and improve the prediction algorithm
model to solve multi-class problems, but it also produced the best results compared to other prediction algorithm
models by using oversampling techniques. with SMOTE, ADASYN and Borderline-SMOTE SVM. In addition,
we also use hyperparameter fine-tuning grids to select appropriate hyperparameters, thereby improving model
evaluation indices including Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score. In the future, we will continue to test many new
types of models as well as find solutions to limit the remaining errors in the results.
